questions.stem: A client obtains a T-score of 70 on a standardized anxiety measure that has a normal distribution. What is the client's approximate percentile rank?
questions.explanation: A T-score of 70 corresponds to 2 standard deviations above the mean (T-scores have M=50, SD=10; so 70 = 50 + 2×10). In a normal distribution, a score at +2 SD corresponds to approximately the 98th percentile. The lesson provides the conversion table showing that +2 SD = T-score of 70 = z-score of +2.0 = percentile rank of 98. Option A (70th) confuses the T-score value with a percentile rank. Option B (84th) corresponds to +1 SD (T-score of 60). Option C (95th) is incorrect and may be confused with the 95% confidence interval.
questions.kn_explanation: Converting T-scores to percentile ranks requires knowledge of probability theory and descriptive statistics (normal distribution properties), which falls under KN55's quantitative analytic methods.
questions.quality_comment: Clean, straightforward conversion question. Tests memorization of the standard score equivalency table which is essential EPPP knowledge.
questions.suggestedParagraph: ### The Critical Table: Standard Score Equivalents

questions.stem: A set of raw scores from an anxiety measure has a bimodal distribution. When each raw score is converted to a z-score using the formula z = (X – M)/SD, the resulting distribution of z-scores will be:
options: Normal, because z-score transformation always normalizes a distribution
Bimodal, because z-score conversion is a linear transformation that preserves the shape of the distribution
Rectangular, because z-scores spread all values evenly across the distribution
Positively skewed, because z-scores shift all values to the right of the distribution
questions.answer: Bimodal, because z-score conversion is a linear transformation that preserves the shape of the distribution
questions.explanation: Converting raw scores to z-scores is a linear transformation, which changes the scale (mean becomes 0, SD becomes 1) but preserves the shape of the original distribution. The lesson explicitly states: 'converting raw scores to standard scores is a linear transformation, the shape of the distribution stays the same. If your raw scores form a bell curve, your standard scores will too.' Therefore, if the original distribution is bimodal, the z-score distribution will also be bimodal. Option A is incorrect — z-score transformation does NOT normalize distributions; that requires a separate nonlinear normalization procedure. Option C describes the effect of converting to percentile ranks, not z-scores. Option D has no basis in how z-score transformation works.
questions.kn_explanation: Understanding z-score transformations and their effect on distribution shape is a descriptive statistics concept, falling under KN55's quantitative analytic methods.
questions.quality_comment: Tests a commonly confused concept — the distinction between linear transformation (z-scores) and nonlinear transformation (percentile ranks/normalization). Strong distractors.
questions.suggestedParagraph: ### Standard Scores: Speaking the Language of Standard Deviations
questions.lessonExcerpt: converting raw scores to standard scores is a linear transformation, the shape of the distribution stays the same. If your raw scores form a bell curve, your standard scores will too.
